1: But in the latter days it shall come to pass that the mountain of the house of the LORD shall be established — ve-hayah be-acharit ha-yamim (in the latter/last days): the eschatological temporal frame, contrasting with the immediate judgment of Micah 3:12. Micah 4:1-3 is nearly identical to Isaiah 2:2-4; whether Micah borrowed from Isaiah or vice versa, or both drew from a common prophetic tradition, is debated. Yihyeh nakhin har-beit YHWH be-rosh he-harim — the mountain of the LORD's house raised as the highest mountain is cosmological symbolism: Zion elevated above all competing centers of gravity, political and religious.
2: Many nations shall come and say, Come, let us go up to the mountain of the LORD — the voluntary pilgrimage of nations to Zion contrasts with the coerced submission of ANE imperial treaties; nations come willingly to learn YHWH's ways. Yamorenu mi-derakhav ve-nelkhah be-orchotav (that he may teach us his ways and we may walk in his paths) — the teaching is practical life-instruction, not abstract theology. Ki mi-Tsiyon tetse Torah (for from Zion shall go forth instruction/Torah) and u-devar-YHWH mi-Yerushalam — Zion as the universal source of teaching, reversing the Babel-scattering pattern.
3: They shall beat their swords into plowshares and their spears into pruning hooks — ve-kittetu charvotem le-ittim ve-chanitoteihem le-mazmeirot: the metalworking reversal image: the forge that produced weapons (charev = sword, chanit = spear) now produces agricultural tools (et = plowshare, mazmeirah = pruning hook). Lo-yissa' goy el-goy cherev ve-lo-yelimdu od milchamah (nation shall not lift up sword against nation, neither shall they learn war anymore) — the cessation of military education and practice; war's institutional structure dismantled.
4: Each one under his vine and under his fig tree — ve-yashvu ish tachat gapno ve-tachat te'enato: the vine-and-fig-tree image is the standard OT shorthand for covenant shalom — self-sufficiency, security, agricultural fruitfulness, absence of fear (1 Kings 4:25; Zechariah 3:10; cf. also the Elijah/juniper contrast in 1 Kings 19:4). Ve-eyn machared (and no one shall make them afraid) — the absence of the terror-by-night characteristic of war-zones. Ki pi YHWH Tseva'ot dibber (for the mouth of the LORD of hosts has spoken) — the divine utterance-formula guarantees the vision.
5: For all the peoples walk each in the name of its god — ki kol-ha-'ammim yelkhu ish be-shem elohav: an honest acknowledgment of present religious pluralism. Va-anachnu nelekh be-shem YHWH Eloheinu le'olam va'ed (but we will walk in the name of the LORD our God forever and ever) — the counter-declaration of covenant fidelity. The verse creates a deliberate contrast between the present (each nation walks its own path) and the eschatological vision (vv1-3, nations streaming to Zion); in the present, Israel's task is faithfulness to YHWH regardless of what others do.
6: In that day, declares the LORD, I will assemble the lame — ha-tsole'ah (the lame) — those who are physically disabled and marginalized. Three groups: tsole'ah (lame), hadde-nichah (driven away/expelled ones), and asher hare'oti (those I have afflicted). The last group is significant: YHWH acknowledges that he himself afflicted some of those he now gathers — divine agency in both the judgment (affliction) and the restoration (gathering).
7: And the lame I will make a remnant — ve-samti et-ha-tsole'ah le-she'erit: the she'erit (remnant) — the core survival concept in the prophets — consists precisely of those who were considered weakest. Ve-ha-nehalah le-goy atzum (and the cast-off ones, a strong nation) — the reversal: the expelled become the strong nation. U-malakh YHWH 'aleihem be-har Tsiyon (and the LORD will reign over them in Mount Zion) — the divine kingship over the gathered remnant; the restored Zion as YHWH's throne.
8: O tower of the flock, hill of the daughter of Zion — Migdal-'Eder (Tower of the Flock) is a specific place name: Genesis 35:21 locates it near Bethlehem, just beyond where Rachel died in childbirth. The shepherd-tower for watching over flocks near Bethlehem becomes a metaphor for the watchpost of Jerusalem's shepherded community. Adah ha-mamlakhah le-vat Yerushalam (the kingdom of the daughter of Jerusalem) — adah (come to, reach) — the former dominion (memshelet ha-rishonah) returns to this center.
9: Now why do you cry aloud? Is there no king in you? — lama atta tari'a: the rhetorical question punctures the lamentation: why the panic? The implied answer: because your king and counselor are gone — military-political leadership has failed. Ki-achazakh chil ke-yoledah (for pain has seized you like a woman in labor) — labor-pain is the signature metaphor for the shock of unexpected disaster (cf. Jeremiah 4:31; Micah 6:24; Micah 30:6). Here it introduces the exile-and-return sequence of vv10-13.
10: Writhe and groan, O daughter of Zion, like a woman in labor — the labor imagery continues: not merely pain but productive pain that leads to birth. Ki-atah tetse'i min-ha'ir ve-shakhan't ba-sadeh (for now you shall go out from the city and dwell in the open country) — the sequence: city → open country → Babylon. Ve-ba't ad-Bavel (you shall go to Babylon) — a remarkable specific prophecy from an 8th century prophet; Micah preaches approximately 100 years before Babylon becomes the dominant power. Sham tinnatseli sham yig'alekh YHWH (there you shall be rescued; there the LORD will redeem you) — rescue in the place of captivity, not from a distance.
11: Now many nations are assembled against you — ve-atah ne'esfu 'alayikh goyim rabbim: the threat from the nations. Their taunt: tetechannaf ve-techaz be-Tsiyon 'eneinu (let her be defiled, and let our eyes gloat over Zion) — techannaf (be defiled, profaned) — the nations want to see Zion desecrated; their gloating gaze is the opposite of the holy pilgrimage of Micah 4:2.
12: But they do not know the thoughts of the LORD — ve-hemma lo yad'u machashevot YHWH: divine intention is hidden from the attacking nations. Ki qibbets otam ka-'amir gornah (for he has gathered them like sheaves to the threshing floor) — the image is judicial-agricultural: YHWH gathers the attacking nations the way a farmer gathers grain, not to save them but to thresh them. The attacking nations think they are victors; they are harvest material.
13: Arise and thresh, O daughter of Zion — qumi ve-dushi bat Tsiyon: after being told to writhe (v10) and seeing the nations gathered (v12), Zion is commanded to thresh. Ki-qarnekh asim barzel ve-parposotayikh asim nechushshah (for I will make your horn iron and your hoofs bronze) — the horn (qeren) and hoofs (parsa) of a threshing animal (ox) made of iron and bronze — divine reinforcement of the human instrument. Ve-hacharamti la-YHWH bilam (and you shall devote their gain to the LORD) — cherem (devoted/consecrated to YHWH) — the spoil is wholly consecrated, not used for self-enrichment.
